g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on Domestic E-Waste Disposal - a critical topic for every CSE student.
E-waste refers to discarded electrical and electronic devices at end-of-life, including computers, phones, TVs, and appliances.
Improper e-waste disposal releases toxic substances like lead and mercury, causing severe health and environmental damage.
Domestic e-waste comes from five main categories: computing devices, communication equipment, entertainment electronics, home appliances, and small gadgets.
E-waste contains toxic substances: lead damages nerves, mercury harms kidneys, cadmium causes bone accumulation, and chromium causes respiratory problems.
Five main disposal methods: formal recycling, EPR take-back programs, donations for reuse, certified collection centers, and manufacturer take-back programs.
The 5-tier hierarchy: Prevention first (reduce purchases), then Reuse (donate/sell), Recycling (process materials), Energy Recovery, and last - secure Disposal.
Formal recycling involves collection, manual dismantling, mechanical shredding, magnetic separation, chemical treatment for metals, and safe residual disposal.
To properly dispose of an old laptop: wipe data, assess for reuse, find collection center, obtain documentation, and never trash it.
For old phones: backup data, factory reset, remove batteries and SIM cards, store safely, and use take-back programs or recycling drives.
India's E-Waste (Management) Rules 2022 mandate EPR - producers must register, set collection targets, and consumers must use authorized channels only.

g.text: Producers responsible for collection & recycling of their products

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: E-waste is toxic, follows management hierarchy, requires proper channels, and data security is crucial for CSE students.
